epigraph.text: Where there is no vision, the people perish.
epigraph.quote: A picture is worth a thousand words.

content.text: {A picture is worth a thousand words.}{Frederick R.\ Barnard}

A graph reveals a function's behavior at a glance—its shape, its 
trends, its critical features.

The most basic method: choose input values, calculate outputs, plot 
the points, connect them.

items: Locate $x = a$ on the horizontal axis

content.title: Reading from graph
content.problem:
From a graph of $f$, if the point $(3, 5)$ is on the graph, then 
$f(3) = 5$.

To find where $f(x) = 2$, locate $y = 2$ and find all $x$-values 
where the graph crosses that horizontal line.

content.title: Intercepts
content.content:
The **$y$-intercept** is where the graph crosses the $y$-axis 
(when $x = 0$). It is the point $(0, f(0))$.

The **$x$-intercepts** (or zeros) are where the graph crosses 
the $x$-axis (when $y = 0$). They are solutions to $f(x) = 0$.

content.title: Finding intercepts
content.solution:
**$y$-intercept:** $f(0) = 0 - 4 = -4$. Point: $(0, -4)$

**$x$-intercepts:** Solve $x^2 - 4 = 0$:
\[
 (x + 2)(x - 2) = 0 \Rightarrow x = -2, x = 2
\]
Points: $(-2, 0)$ and $(2, 0)$

content.title: Increasing and Decreasing
content.content:
A function is **increasing** on an interval if, as $x$ increases, 
$f(x)$ increases (the graph rises from left to right).

A function is **decreasing** on an interval if, as $x$ increases, 
$f(x)$ decreases (the graph falls from left to right).

content.title: Increasing/decreasing
content.problem:
For $f(x) = x^2$:

- Decreasing on $(-\infty, 0)$

- Increasing on $(0, \infty)$
The vertex $(0, 0)$ is where the function changes from decreasing 
to increasing—a minimum point.

content.text:
A **maximum** is the highest point on the graph (or on an interval).
A **minimum** is the lowest point.

items: Opens upward ($a > 0$): vertex is a minimum
Opens downward ($a < 0$): vertex is a maximum

content.title: Even and Odd Functions
content.content:
A function is **even** if $f(-x) = f(x)$ for all $x$. Its graph 
is symmetric about the $y$-axis.

A function is **odd** if $f(-x) = -f(x)$ for all $x$. Its graph 
is symmetric about the origin.

content.title: Even and odd
content.problem:
- $f(x) = x^2$ is even: $f(-x) = (-x)^2 = x^2 = f(x)$

- $g(x) = x^3$ is odd: $g(-x) = (-x)^3 = -x^3 = -g(x)$

- $h(x) = x^2 + x$ is neither

marginNotes.text:
Your word is a lamp for my feet, a 
light on my path.

A time to search and a time to 
give up, a time to keep and a time to throw away.

marginNotes.author: Euclid
marginNotes.text: There is no royal road to geometry.
